title: Algorithm Details
text: site \(\), E-step computes posterior probability occupancy given observed detection history current parameter estimates: \[w_i = P(z_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{y}_i, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{} \sum_j y_{ij} > 0 \text{ (detected)} \\ \frac{\hat{\psi}_i \prod_j (1 - \hat{p}_{ij})}{\hat{\psi}_i \prod_j (1 - \hat{p}_{ij}) + (1 - \hat{\psi}_i)} & \text{} \sum_j y_{ij} = 0 \text{ (undetected)} \end{cases}\] Detected sites always \(w_i = 1\) — species observed least , occupied. Undetected sites \(w_i \(0, 1)\), representing posterior probability occupancy given species never detected. site high occupancy probability \(\hat{\psi}_i\) low detection \(\hat{p}_{ij}\) \(w_i\) close 1 (likely occupied missed). site low \(\hat{\psi}_i\) high \(\hat{p}_{ij}\) \(w_i\) close 0 (likely truly absent). weights direct interpretation soft class assignments. Consider site species never detected. current parameter estimates give site high occupancy probability (say \(\hat{\psi}_i = 0.8\)) low per-visit detection probability (say \(\hat{p}_{ij} = 0.1\) across four visits), probability seeing zero detections given occupancy \((1 - 0.1)^4 = 0.66\). site likely occupied missed, \(w_i\) close 1. Conversely, occupancy low (\(\hat{\psi}_i = 0.1\)) detection high (\(\hat{p}_{ij} = 0.7\)), probability four missed visits given occupancy \((1 - 0.7)^4 = 0.008\). species almost certainly absent, \(w_i\) close 0. E-step computes weights every undetected site, producing soft partition sites occupied unoccupied classes M-step uses regression.

Higher \(M\) means site carries information regression, GLM estimates converge faster EM solution. higher \(M\) also amplifies attenuation problem. EM treats soft weights observed data, regression sees 700/1000 rather hard 0 1. dilutes predictor-response relationship, pulling regression slopes toward zero. effect worse higher \(M\) GLM assigns confidence fractional observation. \(M = 1000\) gives fast convergence (typically 15-25 iterations) attenuation Gibbs correction can fix afterward. title: Algorithm Details
text: Raw E-step weight updates can oscillate, particularly detection probability high many sites near occupancy boundary (\(\hat{\psi}_i \approx 0.5\)). Damping smooths updates: \[w_i^{(t)} = \lambda \cdot w_i^{(t-1)} + (1 - \lambda) \cdot w_i^{(\text{new})}\] \(\lambda\) damping factor: \(\lambda = 0\): fully updated (aggressive, uses new E-step value) \(\lambda = 0.9\): mostly retains old value (conservative) INLAocc uses adaptive damping strategy: Start aggressive: \(\lambda = \max(\text{user\_damp}, 0.1)\) make fast initial progress. Increase oscillating: \(\max|\Delta\psi|\) increases compared previous iteration, \(\lambda\) increased (damping), capped 0.9. Decrease converging: \(\max|\Delta\psi|\) drops 50% compared previous iteration, \(\lambda\) decreased toward floor value allow faster final convergence. prevents divergence difficult regions parameter space allowing rapid convergence objective function well-behaved. practice, damping factor typically starts near 0.1, rises 0.3–0.5 middle iterations, drops back toward 0.1 algorithm converges.

title: Algorithm Details
text: EM converges, short Gibbs sampling chain replaces soft weights hard binary imputations: Gibbs draw produces complete set coefficient estimates model fitted binary (fractional) data. pooled estimates across draws — computed mean draw-specific estimates, standard errors combined via Rubin’s rule — correct attenuation introduced EM soft weights.
code: # Pseudocode for Gibbs data augmentation for (r in seq_len(R)) {   # 1. Draw binary z from the soft weights   z <- rbinom(n_sites, size = 1, prob = w)    # 2. Refit occupancy model with binary z (full INLA or GLM call)   occ_fit <- fit_occupancy(z, occ_covariates)    # 3. Refit detection model conditional on z   det_fit <- fit_detection(y[z == 1, ], det_covariates)    # 4. Store coefficient estimates   store(occ_fit$coefficients, det_fit$coefficients)    # 5. Update w from new estimates for next draw   w <- compute_weights(occ_fit, det_fit) }

title: Formatting Data for INLAocc
text: detection matrix y N rows (sites) J columns (visits): 1 = species detected visit 0 = species detected (visit conducted) NA = visit conducted Key points: Rows always correspond sites columns visits. Variable survey effort encoded naturally: sites fewer visits trailing NA values. matrix must contain 0, 1, NA. Non-integer values, counts greater 1, negative values cause validation errors.
code: set.seed(42) N <- 100   # sites J <- 4     # max visits per site  # Simulate: true occupancy ~ 0.6, detection ~ 0.4 z <- rbinom(N, 1, 0.6)                          # true state y <- matrix(NA_integer_, N, J) for (i in seq_len(N)) {   n_visits <- sample(2:J, 1)                    # variable effort   y[i, 1:n_visits] <- rbinom(n_visits, 1, 0.4 * z[i]) }

title: Formatting Data for INLAocc
text: Multi-season occupancy models track changes occupancy time. detection matrix 3D array dimensions sites x seasons x visits: Covariates can : Static (N-vector): constant across seasons (e.g., elevation). Time-varying (N x n_seasons matrix): changes seasons (e.g., annual land-use change). temporal argument controls temporal correlation structure: "ar1" first-order autoregressive "iid" independent seasons.
code: N <- 50         # sites n_seasons <- 5  # years J <- 3          # visits per season  y_temporal <- array(   rbinom(N * n_seasons * J, 1, 0.3),   dim = c(N, n_seasons, J) ) data_temporal <- list(   y        = y_temporal,   occ.covs = list(     elevation = rnorm(N),                              # static     land_use  = matrix(runif(N * n_seasons), N, n_seasons)  # time-varying   ),   det.covs = list(     effort = array(runif(N * n_seasons * J, 1, 8),                    dim = c(N, n_seasons, J))   ) )  fit_temporal <- occu(~ elevation + land_use, ~ effort, data = data_temporal,                      temporal = "ar1", verbose = 0)

code: N_total <- 150   # total unique sites across all sources  # Source 1: point counts at 80 sites, 4 visits each y_points <- matrix(rbinom(80 * 4, 1, 0.4), 80, 4)  # Source 2: acoustic monitors at 60 sites, 3 visits each y_acoustic <- matrix(rbinom(60 * 3, 1, 0.6), 60, 3)  # 10 sites (71--80) are shared between sources data_integrated <- list(   y = list(     points   = y_points,     acoustic = y_acoustic   ),   occ.covs = data.frame(elevation = rnorm(N_total)),   det.covs = list(     list(effort = matrix(runif(80 * 4, 1, 8), 80, 4)),    # source 1     list(noise  = matrix(rnorm(60 * 3), 60, 3))           # source 2   ),   sites = list(1:80, 71:130),     # which rows of occ.covs each source covers   coords = cbind(x = runif(N_total), y = runif(N_total)) )  fit_integrated <- occu(~ elevation, ~ effort, data = data_integrated,                        integrated = TRUE, verbose = 0)

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: Occupancy models simultaneously estimate two processes: occupancy (species present site?) detection (given presence, observed given visit?). dual-process structure makes diagnostics essential non-trivial. Misspecification either occupancy detection submodel can bias parameters submodels, likelihood entangles two. Traditional residual plots — designed models response observed directly — apply latent state (true presence) never seen. INLAocc addresses simulation-based diagnostics. idea simple: fitted model correct, data simulated look like observed data. systematic discrepancy points specific kind misspecification. approach requires distributional assumptions beyond model , works occupancy model structure — single-season, multi-season, spatial, multi-species. vignette covers full diagnostic model selection toolkit: one-call diagnostic panel (checkModel()), individual hypothesis tests uniformity, dispersion, outliers, zero inflation, spatial temporal autocorrelation diagnostics, DHARMa integration, posterior predictive checks, model comparison via information criteria cross-validation, model averaging, identifiability checks. model fitting basics, see vignette("quickstart"); spatial models specifically, see vignette("spatial-models").

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: simulation-based diagnostics INLAocc follow logic. Given fitted parameters \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\): Simulate latent occupancy states \(z_i^{(r)} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\hat{\psi}_i)\) replicate \(r = 1, \ldots, R\). Simulate detection histories \(y_{ij}^{(r)} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(z_i^{(r)} \cdot \hat{p}_{ij})\). Compute test statistic \(T(\mathbf{y}^{(r)})\) simulated dataset. Compare observed statistic \(T(\mathbf{y})\) distribution \(\{T(\mathbf{y}^{(r)})\}_{r=1}^R\). \(T(\mathbf{y})\) falls tails simulated distribution, model misspecified direction \(T\) measures. Different choices \(T\) target different aspects model fit: variance (dispersion), tail behaviour (outliers), number -zero sites (zero inflation), full distribution (PIT residuals). approach well suited occupancy models standard diagnostic toolkit breaks response latent. logistic regression, observe 0s 1s can compute residuals directly. occupancy model, observe detection histories, occupancy states. site zero detections might unoccupied might occupied low detection probability. way compute residual occupancy without first deciding whether site occupied, quantity trying estimate. Simulation-based diagnostics avoid circularity. require residuals occupancy process . Instead, simulate full generative model: first draw occupancy states fitted occupancy probabilities, draw detection histories states fitted detection probabilities. simulated detection histories compared observed detection histories, discrepancy evaluated terms detection data, observed. second advantage generality. test statistic \(T\) can anything: variance ratio, count zeros, spatial autocorrelation coefficient, quantile distribution. reference distribution \(T\) comes model’s simulations, theoretical distribution assumes particular error structure. means diagnostic framework works single-season models, multi-season models, spatial models, multi-species models without modification. requirement model can simulate , properly specified occupancy model can .

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: checkModel() produces four-panel diagnostic plot gives quick visual overview model adequacy:  four panels : QQ plot PIT residuals. Probability Integral Transform (PIT) residuals transform observed value fitted CDF. true model, residuals follow Uniform(0, 1) distribution, QQ plot track diagonal. S-shaped curve indicates overdispersion (variance model predicts). inverted S indicates underdispersion. systematic shift away diagonal suggests location bias — model consistently - -predicting. Pay attention deviations occur. Departures tails (upper-right lower-left corners QQ plot) indicate problems extreme values: outlier sites model explain, heavy-tailed variation data. Departures middle plot point problems mean structure: model getting central tendency wrong, usually means missing covariate misspecified functional form. Residuals vs fitted. Deviance residuals plotted fitted detection probabilities. formless cloud trends. funnel shape (residuals spreading fitted values increase) indicates heteroscedasticity, meaning variance detection process constant across sites. curved trend suggests missing covariate wrong functional form (e.g., linear term quadratic needed). step pattern, residuals jump particular fitted value, suggests threshold effect data model’s smooth link function capture. Dispersion histogram. variance site-level detection counts computed simulated dataset, producing reference distribution. red vertical line marks observed variance. ratio greater 1.5 suggests overdispersion; 0.7 suggests underdispersion. Overdispersion occupancy models often signals unmodelled heterogeneity: missing random effects, unaccounted spatial structure, covariate groups sites categories different detection rates. adding complexity, check whether single missing covariate explains excess variance. Correlogram. site coordinates available, panel shows Moran’s increasing numbers neighbours. flat line near zero means residual spatial autocorrelation. Peaks small neighbour counts suggest local spatial structure model capturing. checkModel() returns results invisibly, can extract numerical summaries:
code: checkModel(fit) diag <- checkModel(fit) diag$ks_p       # KS test p-value for PIT residuals #> [1] 0.673618 diag$disp_ratio # observed / expected dispersion ratio #> [1] 1.079455

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: Residual spatial autocorrelation means model capturing spatial structure data. test , first simulate spatially structured data fit two models — one ignores space, one accounts : Significant Moran’s confirms non-spatial model leaves spatial structure residuals. Now fit spatial model: Non-significant Moran’s confirms spatial random effect absorbed spatial structure. Two weighting schemes available: weights = "inverse" (default): inverse-distance weights across pairs sites. Sensitive broad-scale spatial structure. weights = "knn" k = 10 (another value): k nearest neighbours. Sensitive local spatial structure.
code: sim_sp <- simulate_occu(N = 300, J = 4, spatial_range = 0.3, seed = 123)  # Non-spatial fit (ignores spatial structure) fit_nospatial <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = sim_sp$data, verbose = 0) moranI(fit_nospatial) #>  #>  Moran's I (inverse-distance weights) #>  #> data:  fit_nospatial #> Moran's I = -0.0037039, Expected I = -0.0033445, p-value = 0.9852 #> alternative hypothesis: two.sided fit_sp <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = sim_sp$data,                spatial = sim_sp$data$coords, verbose = 0) moranI(fit_sp) #>  #>  Moran's I (inverse-distance weights) #>  #> data:  fit_sp #> Moran's I = -0.03101, Expected I = -0.0033445, p-value = 0.1523 #> alternative hypothesis: two.sided moranI(fit_sp, weights = "knn", k = 10) #>  #>  Moran's I (k=10 nearest neighbours) #>  #> data:  fit_sp #> Moran's I = 0.029893, Expected I = -0.0033445, p-value = 0.1728 #> alternative hypothesis: two.sided

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: ppcOccu() implements Bayesian posterior predictive checks (Gelman et al. 2013). computes fit statistic observed data datasets replicated posterior, reports Bayesian p-value: Bayesian p-value near 0.5 indicates good fit — model generates data whose fit statistic distribution centred observed value. Values near 0 1 indicate systematic misfit. fit.stat argument accepts "freeman-tukey" "chi-squared". group argument controls aggregation: group = 1 computes statistic site (sensitive site-level misfit), group = 2 computes visit (sensitive visit-level misfit). choice fit statistic affects kinds misfit detect. Freeman-Tukey sensitive location spread distribution, making good default overall model assessment. Chi-squared emphasizes individual observations deviate strongly expected values, better flagging specific problem sites. well-specified model, statistics give similar Bayesian p-values. Freeman-Tukey passes chi-squared fails, look small number outlier sites rather systematic problem. Use Freeman-Tukey default; switch chi-squared suspect individual sites driving misfit.
code: ppc <- ppcOccu(fit, fit.stat = "freeman-tukey", group = 1) ppc$bayesian.p #> [1] 0

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection
text: Fit set candidate models compare : delta column gives difference WAIC best model. weight gives Akaike-style model weight, interpretable probability model best approximating model given candidate set. WAIC (Widely Applicable Information Criterion) recommended Bayesian models uses full posterior rather point estimate. AIC BIC also available via criterion = "aic" criterion = "bic". WAIC, AIC, BIC answer related distinct questions. WAIC uses full posterior predictive distribution evaluate model fit, means accounts parameter uncertainty. AIC uses maximum likelihood estimate (Bayesian contexts, posterior mode) adds penalty equal number parameters. models simple fixed-effect structures flat priors, WAIC AIC give nearly identical rankings. differences appear model includes informative priors complex random effects. cases, AIC undercounts effective complexity account prior’s regularizing effect, WAIC captures effective number parameters \(p_{\text{WAIC}}\). BIC penalizes complexity heavily AIC, penalty growing \(\log(N)\) instead constant 2 per parameter. large sample sizes, BIC strongly favors simpler models. useful parsimony matters predictive accuracy, instance selecting minimal model population monitoring model applied across many years regions. practice occupancy models: use WAIC default criterion, AIC cross-check (disagree, investigate ), BIC need simplest defensible model. three criteria select model, can confident ranking.

title: Diagnostics & Model Selection

code: sim_bad <- simulate_occu(N = 50, J = 2,                          beta_occ = c(0.5, -0.8),                          beta_det = c(-2, -0.5),  # very low detection                          n_occ_covs = 1, n_det_covs = 1,                          seed = 42)  issues <- checkIdentifiability(sim_bad$data) issues #> Identifiability check: 4 issue(s) found (3 HIGH, 1 MEDIUM) #>  #> [!!] Few visits (J <= 2) #>     J = 2. With 1-2 visits per site, occupancy and detection are confounded. #>     -> Increase survey effort to >= 3 visits per site. #>  #> [!!] Very low naive detection rate #>     Naive p = 0.090. Below 10%, the model struggles to separate absence from non-detection. #>     -> Consider pooling visits, using a simpler detection model, or increasing survey effort. #>  #> [!!] Most sites have no detections #>     42 of 50 sites (84%) have zero detections. #>     -> Very sparse data. Consider whether the species is too rare for occupancy modelling at this scale. #>  #> [!] Low detection history diversity #>     Only 4 unique detection histories across 50 sites. #>     -> Many sites have identical patterns. code: n_sims <- 20 results <- data.frame()  for (i in 1:n_sims) {   sim_i <- simulate_occu(N = 200, J = 4,                          beta_occ = c(0.5, -0.8),                          beta_det = c(0, -0.5),                          n_occ_covs = 1, n_det_covs = 1,                          seed = i)    fit_i <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = sim_i$data, verbose = 0)   cf <- coef(fit_i)    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(     sim = i,     beta0_occ = cf$occ$mean[1],     beta1_occ = cf$occ$mean[2],     alpha0_det = cf$det$mean[1],     alpha1_det = cf$det$mean[2]   )) }  # Estimates should be centered on truth colMeans(results[, -1])

title: Integrated Occupancy Models
text: \(D\) data sources observing set sites, integrated occupancy model shared occupancy layer source-specific detection layers. Occupancy. \(N\) unique sites, latent occupancy state : \[z_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\psi_i), \quad \text{logit}(\psi_i) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}\] \(\mathbf{x}_i\) vector site-level covariates \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) occupancy coefficients. identical standard single-source model — every source shares \(\psi_i\). Detection. source \(d = 1, \ldots, D\) detection process. Source \(d\) surveys subset sites (necessarily \(N\)), surveyed site \(\), makes \(J_d\) visits. observation model : \[y_{ijd} \mid z_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(z_i \cdot p_{ijd}), \quad \text{logit}(p_{ijd}) = \mathbf{w}_{ijd}^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha}_d\] \(\mathbf{w}_{ijd}\) visit-level covariates specific source \(d\), \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_d\) source-specific detection coefficient vector. source intercept, covariates, regression coefficients — reflecting fact , say, camera trap point count fundamentally different detection mechanisms. E-step. EM algorithm updates posterior probability site occupied, conditional observed data. site surveyed sources \(\mathcal{D}_i \subseteq \{1, \ldots, D\}\): \[P(z_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\psi_i \prod_{d \\mathcal{D}_i} \prod_{j=1}^{J_d} p_{ijd}^{y_{ijd}} (1 - p_{ijd})^{1 - y_{ijd}}}{\psi_i \prod_{d \\mathcal{D}_i} \prod_{j=1}^{J_d} p_{ijd}^{y_{ijd}} (1 - p_{ijd})^{1 - y_{ijd}} + (1 - \psi_i) \, \mathbb{}[\mathbf{y}_{\cdot} = \mathbf{0}]}\] product runs sources surveyed site \(\) visits within source. sites least one detection source, posterior 1 (species present). sites zero detections across sources, posterior depends \(\psi_i\) detection probabilities every source. additional source non-detection lowers posterior occupancy probability, giving precise estimates true absence single source alone.

Sources can different numbers sites visits — requirement matrix rows matching corresponding entry sites. occ.covs: data.frame one row per unique site (\(N = 110\) example). occupancy covariates live , shared across sources. det.covs: list \(D\) lists. inner list contains named matrices visit-level detection covariates source. Source 1 date matrix 80 rows 4 columns; source 2 effort matrix 60 rows 10 columns. sites: list \(D\) integer vectors. vector maps rows corresponding y matrix rows occ.covs. example, source 1 covers occ.covs rows 1–80 source 2 covers rows 51–110. title: Random Effects in Occupancy Models
text: Ecological survey data almost always grouped. Sites clustered within regions, visits conducted different observers, species detections depend local conditions vary across spatial administrative units. groups share unmeasured characteristics affect occupancy detection, ignoring grouping structure leads underestimated uncertainty , cases, biased parameter estimates. Random effects provide principled way account structure. random effect allows intercepts slopes vary across groups borrowing strength partial pooling. Instead estimating completely separate parameter group (fixed effects, wasteful many groups) ignoring groups entirely (wrong matter), random effects model assumes group-level deviations drawn common distribution. Groups little data pulled toward overall mean; groups abundant data allowed deviate freely. shrinkage automatic data-driven. INLAocc supports random effects occupancy detection sub-models. formula interface follows syntax lme4 glmmTMB: (1 | group) random intercepts, (x | group) correlated random slopes, (x || group) uncorrelated slopes, (1 | /b) nested grouping. finer control, occu_re() function allows explicit specification latent model prior. random effects estimated via INLA using penalised complexity (PC) priors shrink toward base model (random effect) unless data provide evidence otherwise.

title: Random Effects in Occupancy Models
text: standard occupancy model random intercept occupancy process : \[ \text{logit}(\psi_i) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_{g[]}, \quad u_g \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u) \] \(g[]\) maps site \(\) group (e.g., region, management unit, observer). random effect \(u_g\) shifts baseline log-odds occupancy group. many groups, individual \(u_g\) estimates shrunk toward zero (overall mean link scale), partial pooling property.

title: Random Effects in Occupancy Models
text: effect covariate varies across groups, model becomes: \[ \text{logit}(\psi_i) = \beta_0 + (\beta_1 + v_{g[]}) x_{1i}, \quad v_g \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_v) \] , \(\beta_1\) average effect \(x_1\) across groups, \(v_g\) captures group-specific deviations average. large \(\sigma^2_v\) means covariate effect varies substantially across groups; small \(\sigma^2_v\) means approximately constant.

text: formula syntax covers common cases. need control — non-default latent model, custom prior, sum--zero constraint — use occu_re() directly pass result occ.re det.re argument occu(): model argument accepts INLA latent model name. standard random intercepts, "iid" (independent identically distributed) default. groups natural ordering (e.g., years, age classes), structured models available: "iid" — exchangeable (default). ordering assumed. code: re_spec <- occu_re(   type  = "intercept",   group = "group",   model = "iid",   prior = list(prec = list(prior = "pc.prec", param = c(1, 0.05))) )  fit_explicit <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = sim$data,                      occ.re = re_spec, verbose = 0) # Temporal random effect with AR(1) structure sim$data$occ.covs$year <- rep(2010:2019, each = 30)  re_temporal <- occu_re(   type  = "intercept",   group = "year",   model = "ar1",   prior = list(prec = list(prior = "pc.prec", param = c(1, 0.05))) )  fit_temporal <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = sim$data,                      occ.re = re_temporal, verbose = 0)

title: Spatial Occupancy Models
text: Species detection/non-detection data collected sites distributed across landscape, nearby sites tend share unmeasured environmental conditions. spatial dependence ignored, occupancy models treat sites independent replicates — assumption almost always wrong. consequences real: residual spatial autocorrelation inflates effective sample size, producing confidence intervals narrow fixed-effect estimates may biased. covariate interest spatially structured, bias can substantial. INLAocc handles spatial structure SPDE (Stochastic Partial Differential Equations) approximation continuous Gaussian random field. Rather working dense \(N \times N\) covariance matrix (becomes intractable large \(N\)), SPDE approach represents spatial field piecewise linear function triangulation mesh. yields sparse precision matrix INLA can exploit fast, exact inference. mesh built automatically site coordinates, can construct manually full control. vignette covers full spatial modelling workflow: fitting SPDE models, tuning mesh PC priors, mapping occupancy, predicting new locations, checking spatial residuals, areal (CAR/BYM2) models regional data, spatially varying coefficients, space-time extensions. general model diagnostics beyond spatial context, see vignette("diagnostics").

title: Spatial Occupancy Models
text: PC (penalizing complexity) priors deserve explanation directly influence much spatial structure model captures. prior.range = c(r0, p) encodes \(P(\rho < r_0) = p\), \(\rho\) practical correlation range. sets lower bound spatial scale: believe range unlikely shorter 0.2 (coordinate units), set prior.range = c(0.2, 0.5). tighter prior (e.g., c(0.2, 0.01)) strongly penalises short-range fluctuations. prior.sigma = c(s0, p) encodes \(P(\sigma > s_0) = p\), \(\sigma\) marginal standard deviation spatial field. sets upper bound spatial variance: prior.sigma = c(1, 0.05) means believe spatial SD unlikely exceed 1 logit scale. logic behind PC priors shrinkage toward base model — case, spatial effect (\(\sigma = 0\)). prevents spatial field soaking variation properly belongs fixed-effect covariates. doubt, use weakly informative priors (defaults) let data speak. title: Spatial Occupancy Models
text: sites administrative regions, grid cells, areal units rather point locations, SPDE approach inappropriate. Instead, spatial dependence modelled adjacency structure: regions share border correlated. INLAocc supports areal spatial models via occu_areal(): BYM2 model (Riebler et al., 2016) decomposes spatial effect two components: structured component (conditional autoregressive / CAR): captures spatially smooth variation, region’s value depends neighbours. unstructured component (IID): captures region-specific noise spatially structured. mixing parameter \(\phi \[0, 1]\): controls proportion variance attributable structured vs. unstructured component. Values near 1 indicate strong spatial structure; near 0 means variation essentially independent across regions. occu_areal() accepts three adjacency formats: symmetric binary matrix, spdep::nb neighbourhood object, path INLA graph file. Use whichever convenient workflow. simpler "besag" model (pure CAR, unstructured component) also available BYM2 generally preferred handles disconnected graphs provides structured/unstructured decomposition.
code: # Adjacency matrix: 1 if regions share a border, 0 otherwise adj <- matrix(0, 100, 100) # ... fill from shapefile or raster neighbourhood ...  areal_spec <- occu_areal(adj, model = "bym2")  fit_areal <- occu(~ occ_x1, ~ det_x1, data = my_data,                   spatial = areal_spec, verbose = 0) summary(fit_areal)

title: Spatial Occupancy Models
text: standard spatial model assumes covariate effects \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) constant across study area, spatial field capturing residual structure. sometimes covariate’s effect genuinely varies space — example, elevation may matter occupancy one region another. spatially varying coefficient (SVC) model allows : \[\text{logit}(\psi_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \tilde{\beta}_1(\mathbf{s}_i) \, x_{1i}\] \(\tilde{\beta}_1(\mathbf{s})\) spatial random field slope \(x_1\). total effect \(x_1\) site \(\) \(\beta_1 + \tilde{\beta}_1(\mathbf{s}_i)\): global mean effect plus spatially varying departure. INLAocc, svc argument specifies occupancy coefficient varies spatially, indexed position design matrix (1 = intercept, 2 = first covariate, etc.): spatially varying intercept (svc = 1) equivalent standard SPDE model — spatial field modulates baseline occupancy. spatially varying slope (svc = 2) asks specific question: effect occ_x1 change across space?

title: Migrating from spOccupancy
text: spOccupancy (Doser et al. 2022) standard R package Bayesian occupancy models. covers single-species, multi-species, spatial, temporal, integrated designs, fitted via Polya-Gamma MCMC. used spOccupancy model types, already know data format modelling workflow INLAocc builds . INLAocc replaces MCMC backend INLA (integrated nested Laplace approximation), analytic approximation posterior avoids sampling entirely. user-facing consequence speed: models take minutes MCMC finish seconds INLA, burn-, thinning, convergence diagnostics. statistical consequence posteriors approximate rather exact — well-identified models moderate large data, difference negligible (benchmark correlations INLA MCMC posteriors exceed 0.99). vignette covers everything need move existing spOccupancy workflow INLAocc: data compatibility, function mapping, side--side code examples, posterior sample access, diagnostics equivalences, honest caveats MCMC remains better choice. model-specific details, see vignette("spatial-models"), vignette("multi-species"), vignette("diagnostics").

title: Migrating from spOccupancy
text: Speed. INLAocc 5–20x faster spOccupancy across model types. 300 sites 4 visits, spatial occupancy model runs 15–30 seconds instead 3–5 minutes. 1000 sites, 1–2 minutes instead 20–30 minutes. gap widens dataset size INLA scales sublinearly number sites. Deterministic results. EM-INLA algorithm converges point estimate posterior approximation every run. chain--chain variability, need set random seeds reproducibility. MCMC tuning. spOccupancy requires setting n.batch, batch.length, n.burn, n.thin, n.chains, n.neighbors, cov.model — tuning decision affects results runtime. INLAocc equivalent parameters. Fewer researcher degrees freedom means fewer opportunities accidental p-hacking posterior summaries. Unified interface. One function (occu()) handles 17 model types via keyword arguments. need remember whether spatial multi-species version spMsPGOcc() stMsPGOcc(). switch. Three situations favour staying spOccupancy: need exact posterior samples custom derived quantities go beyond predict() provides. INLAocc generates posterior samples via INLA’s Gaussian approximation, accurate marginals capture full joint posterior. need informative inverse-Gamma priors specific variance components. INLAocc uses PC priors (penalizing complexity), conceptually different easily mapped inverse-Gamma hyperparameters. INLA approximation quality matters problem: small datasets (< 30 sites), weakly identified models (many covariates, low detection probability), models posterior skewness scientifically important.

title: Spatially Varying Coefficient Models
text: Standard occupancy models assume covariate effect everywhere: one slope, applied uniformly across sites. practice, ecological relationships often spatially non-stationary. Elevation might drive occupancy strongly one region little effect another. Forest cover might positively associated occurrence near range centre negatively associated range edges species already marginal. constant-coefficient model forced onto data kind spatial heterogeneity, global estimate compromise accurately describes region particular. title: Multi-Season Occupancy Models
text: Many ecological studies span multiple years seasons. species occupies site one year may go locally extinct next, previously unoccupied sites may colonized. Single-season occupancy models treat site snapshot, discarding temporal information embedded repeated surveys across years. data contain temporal structure, ignoring wastes information best produces biased estimates worst — occupancy trends confounded detection variation, standard errors narrow model treats non-independent observations independent. Multi-season (dynamic) occupancy models address decomposing temporal variation structured process. classical formulation (MacKenzie et al. 2003), colonization extinction rates modelled explicitly. INLAocc takes different approach: models temporal dynamics AR(1) random effect occupancy, captures persistence structure (high occupancy tends follow high occupancy) without requiring separate colonization/extinction parameters. parsimonious goal inference occupancy probability drivers rather transition rates . INLAocc fits multi-season models stacking site-period combinations joint occupancy model AR(1) temporal random effect. Detection fit per-period, season gets detection parameters — survey conditions July 2019 need resemble July 2023. temporal random effect \(\eta_t\) captures shared year--year fluctuation: good years (favourable weather, high resource availability) raise occupancy across sites, bad years lower . Fixed effects occupancy detection estimated simultaneously, AR(1) structure absorbing residual temporal autocorrelation otherwise inflate standard errors.

title: Multi-Season Occupancy Models
text: site \(\) time period \(t\), occupancy process : \[z_{} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\psi_{}), \quad \text{logit}(\psi_{}) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + \eta_t\] \(z_{}\) true (latent) occupancy state \(\psi_{}\) occupancy probability. temporal random effect follows AR(1) process: \[\eta_t = \rho \cdot \eta_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_\eta)\] \(\rho \(-1, 1)\) temporal autocorrelation parameter \(\sigma^2_\eta\) innovation variance. observation process (within period) : \[y_{ijt} \mid z_{} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(z_{} \cdot p_{ijt}), \quad \text{logit}(p_{ijt}) = \mathbf{w}_{ijt}^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha}\] \(y_{ijt}\) detection/non-detection outcome site \(\), visit \(j\), period \(t\), \(p_{ijt}\) detection probability. Three features formulation worth highlighting: \(\eta_t\) captures shared temporal trends. sites experience temporal random effect given period. represents “good years vs. bad years” species — shared environmental conditions raise lower occupancy across landscape. \(\rho\) controls persistence. high \(\rho\) (e.g., 0.8) means occupancy patterns persist across periods: year good, next year likely good . low \(\rho\) means year nearly independent last. Detection varies across periods. detection covariates \(\mathbf{w}_{ijt}\) can include period-specific variables (e.g., survey date, observer effort, weather surveys). period’s detection model estimated period’s data, changing survey protocols across years handled naturally. \(\rho = 0.7\) mean ecological terms? means occupancy average year, 70% excess carries next year. site network occupied sites remain occupied empty sites remain empty produces high \(\rho\). typical species strong site fidelity slow population dynamics — think territorial birds, long-lived amphibians, perennial plants. low \(\rho\) (say 0.2) indicates knowing year’s occupancy tells little next year: species may nomadic, local populations may driven stochastic events like flooding fire. innovation standard deviation \(\sigma_\eta\) captures magnitude year--year environmental stochasticity accounting temporal persistence. large \(\sigma_\eta\) high \(\rho\) means system strong shocks persist (bad year stays bad several years). large \(\sigma_\eta\) low \(\rho\) means large transient fluctuations. classical colonization-extinction parameterization (MacKenzie et al. 2003) models persistence explicit transition probabilities: colonization \(\gamma\) extinction \(\epsilon\). formulations capture temporal dependence, AR(1) parsimonious colonization extinction rates inferential target. need estimate quickly empty sites recolonized, dynamic parameterization natural. need estimate occupancy trends drivers, AR(1) typically sufficient avoids identifiability issues arise \(\gamma\) \(\epsilon\) interact covariates.

title: Multi-Season Occupancy Models
text: Multi-season data INLAocc uses 3D array dimensions sites \(\times\) periods \(\times\) visits: entry 1 (detected), 0 (detected), NA (visit conducted). example single site surveyed 5 years 3 visits per year: Covariates follow dimensional logic: occ.covs: Site-level covariates can either length-\(N\) vector (static across periods, e.g., elevation) \(N \times T\) matrix (time-varying, e.g., yearly temperature anomaly). Static covariates recycled across periods internally. det.covs: Visit-level covariates \(N \times T \times J\) arrays. example, Julian survey date varies site, year, visit. coords: \(N \times 2\) matrix site coordinates, constant across periods. Used spatial component added (see Section 8). Missing visits (NA detection array) handled automatically. Entire missing periods site (visits NA) effectively remove site-period combination likelihood. Common temporal data collection designs map onto structure different ways. Annual surveys (e.g., breeding bird surveys repeated yearly) produce one period per year, visits replicate surveys within breeding season. Seasonal surveys (spring autumn counts within year) can treated separate periods occupancy expected change seasons, additional visits within single period species’ presence assumed stable within year. --control-impact (BACI) designs fit naturally 3D array, period index spans pre- post-intervention years. key question whether species’ true occupancy state can change two time points: yes, separate periods; , replicate visits within period.

title: INLAocc: Occupancy Models via INLA
text: Occupancy models separate two processes: species occurs detect . Fitting MCMC slow, especially spatial multi-species structure. sites never detected species, don’t know absent just missed. MCMC handles sampling latent states directly. INLAocc instead alternates two steps: given current estimates, compute likely undetected site truly occupied (E-step); refit occupancy detection models INLA using weights (M-step). estimates converge iterations. Plain EM (Dempster, Laird & Rubin, 1977) treats site’s occupancy weight fixed estimating coefficients. soft weights attenuate occupancy detection coefficients toward zero. MCMC avoids sampling full present/absent vector iteration fitting coefficients conditional . INLAocc recovers Gibbs-style data augmentation step EM converges. correction alternates sampling hard binary occupancy states converged posterior weights, refitting occupancy model sampled states, refitting detection model subset sites sampled occupied. short burn-, chain stabilizes draws pooled using Rubin’s combining rules (Rubin, 1987). Data Augmentation algorithm (Tanner & Wong, 1987) INLA conditional sampler; using debias EM-based occupancy models , knowledge, novel. submodel fitted INLA (Rue, Martino & Chopin, 2009), Bayesian method: returns full posterior marginals every parameter, point estimates. EM approximation enters latent occupancy states handled; Gibbs correction removes bias. benchmarks, corrected estimates correlate > 0.99 full MCMC posteriors.

title: INLAocc: Occupancy Models via INLA
text: MCMC runtime grows linearly number sites. INLAocc grows sublinearly EM iteration splits occupancy model two independent GLMMs, INLA solves near-linear time via sparse precision matrices. Gibbs debiasing step adds constant number INLA refits preserves scaling advantage. MCMC factorize way must jointly sample latent states alongside parameters. multi-species models advantage compounds , since INLAocc fits species independently MCMC must sample full community one chain.

(simulation-based, on residuals) testUniformity(fit)                # are residuals uniformly distributed? testDispersion(fit)                # more/less variance in residuals than expected? testOutliers(fit)                  # sites outside the simulation envelope? testZeroInflation(fit)             # more unoccupied sites than the model predicts?  # Is there leftover spatial or temporal structure in residuals? moranI(fit)                        # Moran's I on occupancy residuals durbinWatson(fit)                  # lag-1 autocorrelation across time periods variogram(fit)                     # semivariance of residuals vs distance  # One-call panel: QQ plot, residuals vs fitted, dispersion, correlogram checkModel(fit)  # Before fitting: flag identifiability problems in the data checkIdentifiability(dat) checkIdentifiability(fit)          # also works post-fit (boundary estimates, collapsed REs)

what: INLAocc: Occupancy Models via Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation — INLAocc-package
title: INLAocc: Occupancy Models via Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation — INLAocc-package
text: Fits single-species, multi-species, spatial, temporal, integrated, spatially-varying coefficient occupancy models using Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations (INLA). Provides fast alternative MCMC-based frameworks like 'spOccupancy', compatible API including mixed-model random effects syntax, posterior predictive checks, WAIC, k-fold cross-validation, design-matrix prediction. Uses EM algorithm INLA M-step two-process (occupancy/detection) likelihood.

title: INLAocc: Occupancy Models via Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation — INLAocc-package
text: Maintainer: Gilles Colling gilles.colling051@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder]

title: Fit occupancy models using INLA — occu
text: occ.formula RHS formula occupancy (psi). Supports mixed-model random effects syntax: ~ elev + forest + (1 | region). det.formula RHS formula detection (p). NULL JSDM models (set multispecies = "jsdm"). data occu_data occu_data_ms object, raw list components y, occ.covs, det.covs (spOccupancy-compatible). spatial N x 2 coordinate matrix occu_spatial() object. Enables spatial SPDE models. temporal "ar1" "iid" multi-season models. NULL (default) single-season. multispecies FALSE (default), TRUE community models, "jsdm" joint species distribution models (detection process). integrated TRUE multi-data-source models. Requires data$y list detection matrices. n.factors Integer number latent factors. Enables latent factor variants multi-species models. svc Integer vector occupancy design matrix columns get spatially-varying coefficients (1 = intercept). Requires spatial. spde.args List arguments passed occu_spatial() spatial coordinate matrix. priors occu_priors() object spOccupancy-compatible named list. occ.re Explicit list occu_re() specs occupancy. det.re Explicit list occu_re() specs detection. max.iter Maximum EM iterations (default 50). tol Convergence tolerance (default 1e-4). damping EM damping factor 0–1 (default 0.3). ensemble Logical; TRUE, use ensemble averaging across multiple imputation chains (default FALSE). num.threads Thread specification INLA, ":B" outer B inner OpenMP threads (default "1:1"). k.fold Number cross-validation folds (default 0, CV). verbose 0 = silent, 1 = iteration summaries, 2 = full INLA output.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: Damping starts aggressive adapts: backs oscillating (delta_psi increases), accelerates converging fast (delta_psi halves). user-supplied damping sets floor.

what: Stochastic EM (VB E-step replacement) ✗ — cut after testing
title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: Attempted: sampling hard z’s Bernoulli(w) EM iteration. Failed due absorbing state: extreme psi estimates → near-zero sampling weights → z=0 → extreme psi. occupancy model’s two-process structure makes within-EM stochastic sampling unstable. MI debiasing post-convergence remains correct approach — uses hard-z sampling converged weights, avoiding absorbing state.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: occu_areal(adj, model = "bym2") creates areal spatial effects adjacency matrices, spdep::nb objects, INLA graph files. Alternative SPDE grid/administrative unit data.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: resolve_group_vals() handles interaction groups (site:year) constructing factors component columns. Formula syntax (1|site) + (1|observer) already worked crossed via independent f() terms; nested (1|/b) now correctly resolves.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: MI now runs K=20 (configurable) stops early -imputation variance stabilizes (relative change < 5%). Minimum K=5 checking. Saves 30-50% MI compute well-identified models.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: engine_ms now uses parallel::parLapply options(INLAocc.cores = N) set, matching engine_ms_temporal’s parallelism. Also cleaned hardcoded devtools path temporal engine.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: occu(..., ensemble = TRUE) uses pool_community_ensemble(): fits community estimates B=50 random species subsets (70% ), pools across subsets robustness outlier species.

title: INLAocc — Implemented Improvements
text: occu(..., num.threads = "2:4") passes INLA calls. Default remains "1:1" reproducibility.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: Initial release.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: EM-INLA algorithm scaled-binomial occupancy M-step. Novel EM + multiple imputation hybrid beta debiasing (cor > 0.99 MCMC). Adaptive damping GLM warm start convergence stability. Unified occu() API dispatching model types.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: Single-species: occu(), occu_inla(). Spatial SPDE: occu(..., spatial = coords), spatial_occu_inla(). Multi-season AR(1): occu(..., temporal = "ar1"), temporal_occu_inla(). Multi-species community: occu(..., multispecies = TRUE), ms_occu_inla(). Latent factor multi-species: lfMsOccu_inla(), sfMsOccu_inla(). Spatially-varying coefficients: svcOccu_inla(). Integrated multi-source: intOccu_inla(), spIntOccu_inla(). Joint SDM (detection): lfJSDM_inla().

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: Native AST-based formula parser (1 | group), (x | group), (x || group) syntax. Supports nested grouping (1 | /b), operator expansion (*b | group). Pure AST walker — regex deparsed code.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: ppcOccu(): posterior predictive checks (Freeman-Tukey, chi-squared). waicOccu(): WAIC model comparison (per-species .sp). fitted(), residuals(): S3 methods model types. simulate(): posterior predictive simulation (site-level observation-level). pitResiduals(): PIT scaled residuals integer randomisation. testUniformity(): KS test PIT residuals. testDispersion(): simulation-based /underdispersion test. testOutliers(): simulation envelope exceedance test. testZeroInflation(): observed vs expected zero-detection sites. moranI(): Moran’s inverse-distance (default) k-NN weights. durbinWatson(): Durbin-Watson test temporal autocorrelation. variogram(): empirical semivariogram plot() method. checkModel(): one-call 2x2 diagnostic panel (QQ, residuals, dispersion, correlogram). dharma(): optional DHARMa convenience bridge.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: compare_models(): AIC, BIC, WAIC delta Akaike/Schwarz weights. modelAverage(): Burnham & Anderson full model averaging unconditional SEs. AIC(), BIC() via logLik() correct df nobs attributes.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: coef(), confint(), vcov(), nobs(), logLik(). update() formula modification support. tidy(), glance(): broom-compatible output. ranef(): random effect posterior summaries. summary(), print(), plot() model types.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: predict() ggpredict-style terms argument. Design-matrix prediction via X.0, X.p.0, coords.0. predict_spatial(): convenience wrapper spatial interpolation. marginal_effect(): single-covariate response curves. richness(): site-level species richness multi-species models.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: occu_format(): spOccupancy-compatible data validation. occu_data(): pipe-friendly data construction long-format data.frames. occu_format_ms(): multi-species data formatting.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
text: postHocLM(): Bayesian frequentist linear model estimated parameters. getSVCSamples(): extract spatially-varying coefficient posteriors.

title: INLAocc 0.1.0
